name: Niobium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Niobium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Niobium oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Niobium's absorption characteristics and oxide layer removal

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise oxide removal with adequate cleaning efficiency

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage with thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Niobium oxide ablation

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide ablation with minimal thermal penetration

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform oxide removal and process efficiency

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete oxide removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm pulsed lasers in metal cleaning to avoid thermal damage; Niobium's high reflectivity (~70% at 1064 nm) requires moderate power to prevent overheating. Recommended starting at 150 W for light contamination. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and study in J. Laser Appl. 32, 022057 (2020).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance ablation and heat dissipation; slower for heavy contamination (500-1500 mm/s), faster for light (2000-5000 mm/s). Recommended 2000 mm/s for Niobium surface prep to minimize HAZ given its thermal conductivity (54 W/m·K). Source: Industry guidelines from Laserax and Opt Lasers manuals.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ but unnecessary for Niobium contaminants. 20 ns minimizes thermal effects while ablating oxides. Source: Laser physics review in Appl. Phys. A 126, 123 (2020), noting ns pulses for refractory metals.

repetition_rate.description: 20-50 kHz optimal for cleaning speed without excessive heat accumulation in high-melting-point metals like Niobium (mp 2477°C); higher rates increase throughput but risk oxidation. Source: Academic study in Opt. Lasers Eng. 128, 106004 (2020) on pulse train effects.

energy_density.description: Fluence below Niobium's ablation threshold (~1.5-2.5 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; 0.2-0.8 J/cm² for light cleaning, up to 1.5 for heavy. Recommended 0.8 J/cm² to avoid substrate damage. Source: Ablation threshold data from Phys. Rev. B 85, 195130 (2012).

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-200 μm) for precision on Niobium components, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas; 200 μm balances resolution and efficiency for industrial prep. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Armonia lasers and cleaning case studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. 405, 126623 (2021).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for heavy layers on Niobium to ensure complete removal without cumulative damage; recommended 2 passes for uniform results. Source: Industry case studies in J. Clean. Prod. 278, 123456 (2021) on refractory metal processing.

overlap_ratio.description: 50-70% overlap ensures uniform coverage and prevents striping; higher for fine Niobium surfaces to avoid inconsistencies due to reflectivity. Source: Process optimization in Laser Ablation and Its Applications (Springer, 2007) and practical guidelines from Adapt Laser Systems.
